replies.title: Re: Order of Essenes breathing instructions
replies.content:
In vocal music training, a similar-sounding set of breathing instructions is one of the first techniques students are asked to practice. In that case, if you are lying on your back, the area moves up towards the ceiling. (My teacher had students actually place a book there to learn to make it lift with the inhale.) It feels counter-intuitive at first, but the aspiring singer is learning to pull the diaphragm muscle down into the belly, moving the organs there outward (if you are standing) or upward if lying down. This is to counter the habit of chest breathing, inhaling high in the chest first and sucking the belly in toward the backbone on the inhale, which allows a much smaller volume to enter the lungs (a shallower breath) than the aforementioned belly breathing. I haven't read the Essene materials, but I am guessing the principles may be similar.
--Heather in CA

comments.title: The telluric current
comments.content:
Hello, Ynnothir here.

Given your new project on telluric magic, this seems like a good time to finally ask about something that's perplexed me for a while.

First some relevant background. I studied jujitsu formally for several years during the 90's and kept practicing through the the mid 00's. At that point I was forced to stop by a rare autoimmune/neuromuscular disorder.(My immune system is attacking specifically and only the myelin sheathing of my peripheral motor neurons. It's called MMN, for the curious.)

My sensei didn't teach us anything about ki. He would answer direct questions about it in a concise and neutral manner and that would be that. However, at some point during my study of "the art of flowing", I became aware of its influence and started putting it to use.

That was fortunate, because now my lower legs are like toothpicks and that's where balance is brought to bear.

But I've never fallen, despite the fact that I share my home with a number of cats who love affection and don't really grasp how bipedalism works. (My neurologist doesn't even ask about falling anymore) That's because I have this trick where I blow ki out of the palms of my hands like highly maneuverable, independently operating turbofans. Part of the balancing effect comes from the weight of my arms shifting my center of gravity, of course, but that's not even half of it. I should know; I've been a jujitsuka for twenty-six years and the owner/operator of a human body for at least twice that long.

Now here's where I can't really fit things together conceptually. When I gather solar energy I can feel, usually, a slight warming in my palms. This is whether I'm inside or out. That seems about right; this is an astral current, and it's not necessarily something you'll even feel at all. But when I draw on the telluric current it feels like ki. I mean, there ain't no maybe sí, maybe no, I could about put this stuff in my mouth and chew it. Doesn't that mean it's etheric? And yet everything I find in the literature about the solar and telluric currents seems to consider them to be complimentary aspects of the astral plane.

Can you help me make sense of this?

Many thanks

-- Ynnothir Coll

replies.title: Re: The telluric current
replies.content: Sure. Remember that each plane of existence tends to shape the plane below it. The telluric current is astral, but it's pulling etheric energy along with it. You might consider deliberately trying to draw ki into your palms from the sun when you absorb the solar current, and see what results you get.

comments.title: Relationships with the opposite sex
comments.content: Is there a divination or other method to determine if you have blockage preventing you from finding someone appropriate for a romantic relationship?  I suffer from the common lament of not being able find anyone with whom I have much in common.  I can meet women no problem, but meeting someone who holds my interest is another story.  I've prayed on it, and the Goddess who looks after me helps me in innumerable ways, but not [yet] this one.  Any  way to determine if I am self-sabotaging or being repellent somehow?  If there is, that would give me a starting point to work from.

replies.title: Re: Relationships with the opposite sex
replies.content:
This is an extraordinarily challenging time for human relationships, and that has to be factored in to any question of this kind. Still, I think you're partly answering your own question. The difficulty, as you've indicated, is that the women you meet don't hold your interest. What exactly are you looking for, that the women you meet don't fit? And why is that so important that you're not willing to have a relationship if it's not present?

This is the kind of question that's often best approached through journaling. Get a blank notebook and a pen, and start a conversation with yourself about the relationship you want, the women you know, and so on. Ask yourself questions and then write down whatever comes to mind, without censoring anything, no matter how absurd or unpleasant it might be. Give this fifteen minutes or so a day until you have a pretty clear idea of what's going on inside your head; then put it away for a month or two; then take it out again and do some more journaling. Most of us have a lot of oddities packed away in our thought processes; getting them out into the open is an important first step in self-knowledge, which is essential for successful magic.

replies.title: Re: Relationships with the opposite sex
replies.content:
I would also add to the excellent advice JMG gives below: it may turn out that you're genuinely not interested in a romantic relationship right now (or ever) and only want one because society says you're supposed to, or you like the idealized images presented but something in your subconscious is aware that the real thing wouldn't suit you, or whatnot.

That may not be the case, but a) it's worth asking *why* you want a relationship as well as what kind you want or who with, and b) if your journaling actually leads you in a "nah, not for me, actually" direction, that's totally fine. Plenty of people are happy without that sort of pairing.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Can you recommend some good books or other resources about alchemy?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Sure. The best way to start is to get some basic knowledge of spagyrics, the branch of alchemy that deals with herbs; the spagyric literature is fairly sane, which is more than can be said about most other modern writings on the subject! Having some experience with hands-on alchemical work also makes it much easier to make sense of the texts; Jung would have written far fewer absurdities on the subject if he'd spent some time practicing some basic form of laboratory alchemy.

Mark Stavish's book The Path of Alchemy is a very solid introduction to spagyrics, and the book I recommend to beginners; other important spagyric texts are The Alchemist's Handbook by Frater Albertus and Spagyrics by Manfred Junius. Meanwhile, for a broader education on the subject, Adam McLean's gargantuan alchemy website is your go-to place. The old alchemists used to say Lege, lege, lege, relege, ora, labora, ut invenies -- "read, read, read, reread, pray, work, and you will discover."

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'm curious, from your perspective, what one example of an absurdity that Jung wrote on the subject of alchemy might be. Jung focused on the subjective or psychological processes associated with alchemy. Is this what you mean, or something else? Thanks.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Jung actually has a lot of useful things to say about psychological alchemy, which is one branch or subset of the alchemical sciences. The absurdities showed up when he convinced himself that alchemy was only about psychology, and that the alchemists were merely projecting psychological factors onto the contents of their lab glassware. A few months spent macerating, calcining, cohobating, and projecting spagyric products would have taught him that psychological alchemy is only one branch of the Royal Art, and that there are also physical alchemies.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Is it known how ancient people originally learned how to do magic? For example I'm thinking of the archeology programmes I've seen about standing stones. I don't remember them ever talking about evidence stones were pulled back up and moved to new locations, which you would expect if they were testing things and learning from experience.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
One wouldn't move huge stone blocks without a very good reason--the same that made it unlikely to change stuff. These projects were also "expensive", in terms of the resources beyond survival allocated to them. And when the societies that built them collapsed, there was no way anymore to do any changes to them.

Anything smaller might have been disassembled and repurposed, which would explain the lack of smaller scale examples.

Talking with spirits and experimentation account for the building of knowledge on magic. The problems to dismantle the larger structures possibly account for their survival bias.

Sudden revolutions against exploitation might have done abrupt stopping to the use of temples (it is already bad to think of the cost of building them, with the consequences of the use being possibly worse, given that humans tend to turn into corruption anything they touch. Think Pharaoh being buried with all his servants).

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Human beings biologically identical to you and me have been around now for some 315,000 years; recorded history goes back about 5,000 years. Every human society of which there's any record has had rich traditions of magic, so it's a safe bet that magic was invented well back along that 315,000-year timespan and had plenty of time to develop before the oldest magical artifacts we now have. So we have no idea how magic originated, any more than we know how hominids first figured out how to light fires or hunt large game.

As for the standing stones, though, we do in fact have evidence that they were moved repeatedly. Get a good book about Stonehenge, and you'll find that there are holes all over the site where wooden posts were set up and then taken down; when the bluestones arrived some centuries later, they were set up in one pattern, then almost immediately taken down and arranged in the pattern they now have.

replies.title: Re: Novel gods
replies.content:
I can't speak to the former question (well, aside from "spend twenty years building your own mythology from scratch", and I'm not sure how good of a job I've done), but I can attest that the latter can happen. Doesn't even take getting really popular; the character/culture hero/goddess who made it clear to me she existed is one I hadn't talked about to anyone else. (She was quite cross at me, actually; I didn't meet her standards.)

You might nose around the anime Puella Magi Madoka Magica one of these days, or at least the fandom for it; I rather suspect that some or all of the main five girls (who the fandom, perhaps more accurately than they intended, call the "Holy Quintet") are examples of this.

replies.title: Re: Novel gods
replies.content:
1) Start by doing some serious reading in three or four mythologies about which you know absolutely nothing. They should come from three or four different corners of the world. Get a sense of how the myths work, how the gods divide up the various divine functions, and so forth; then come up with something that isn't similar to any of the ones you've read, but differs from them the way that they differ from each other. That's how the best fantasy mythologies came into being -- it was, for example, Tolkien's method in creating the Valar.

2)They'd be praying to an egregor to start with, but very likely something would show up to make use of the opportunity. The deity would probably start off behaving as per the novel, but might change his or her habits over time, the way deities generally do.

Yes. The standard hoodoo protective bath is a good starting place: half a tub of water as cool as you can handle it, a quarter cup of kosher salt, a quarter cup of vinegar, and a pinch of cayenne pepper. Stand in the tub, and with a clean cloth, wash downward from above. When you're done, towel off and get dressed, and take a pailful of the water from the bath and throw it on the roots of the biggest, healthiest tree you can find. Do this at least once a week; you can do it daily if you need to.

Second, a protective amulet. Get a little red cloth bag on a cord, sized so that you can wear it around your neck. (You didn't mention your gender; if you're a woman, you can also simply tuck it down the center of your bra.) Into the bag goes half a teaspoon or so of kosher salt, and a bright iron or steel nail (i.e., white metal, not brass) that you've bent at an angle with two pairs of plyers. Wear that whenever you're going be around the people in question, as close as possible to your heart. The iron discharges certain kinds of nasty magical energies; the salt absorbs the discharges so they don't affect you.

Those will give you protection immediately, which a course of study won't. If you decide you want to study magic in order to learn how to protect yourself from toxic energies, well, depending on what your spiritual beliefs are, I may be able to recommend a book or two... ;-)

comments.title: Scrambled Frog Eggs
comments.content:
Hi John & all,

Ohio State Press recently published some writings of William S. Burrough's that have been around, but not necessarily available in full, or in this form. The book in question is titled "The Revised Boy Scout Manual: an electronic revolution." I was struck by how prescient this piece of text in it is, specifically to the tactics of 4-chan chaos magicians. (Of course Burroughs was a huge influence on the chaos magic scene.) He was writing about fake news decades before fake news. And this seems so close to the youtube type video editing as done on the chans and other online spaces. I also thought this was interesting in regards to your recent post here on the failure of other political magics.

"For a start you scramble the news all together and spit it out every which way on ham radio and street recorders. You construct fake news broadcasts on video camera... .And you scramble your fabricated news in with actual news broadcasts.

You have an advantage which your opposing player does not have. He must conceal his manipulations. You are under no such necessity. In fact you can advertise the fact that you are writing news in advance and trying to make it happen by techniques which anybody can use.

And that makes you NEWS. And a TV personality as well, if you play it right. You want the widest possible circulation for your cutup video tapes. Cutup techniques could swamp the mass media with total illusion.

The simplest experiment consists in playing back a scrambled message to subject. Message could contain simple commands. Does the scrambled message have any command value comparable to post-hypnotic suggestion? Is the actual content of the message received?

Let us say the message is fear. For this we take all the past fear shots of the subject we can collect or evoke. We cut these in with fear words and pictures, with threats, etc. This is all acted out and would be upsetting enough in any case. Now let’s try it scrambled and see if we get an even stronger effect."

Do you have any thoughts on this practice as a magical technique?

Justin Patrick Moore

replies.title: Re: Scrambled Frog Eggs

comments.title: Subtle properties or plastic, and other materials
comments.content:
Hello JMG, CR Patino here.

First of all, Thank you very much for sharing your wisdom in this space.

It's been a while since you have discussed the "dead energies" of items made of plastic. My understanding is that using plastic vs wood (or other innert materials such as glass) is about as the difference between eating rotten flesh vs the still fresh meat of a dead animal. Both will provide the raw calories your body needs to sustain itself, but the former will have unwelcome side effects that your organism will have to work very hard to overcome.

The question is, what is the difference between this modern plastic material vs other dead materials (known and hopefully well documented by the Occult Traditions of times past)? I am thinking specifically about bone items, that all cultures have crafted and used for a long time, even after materials with better physical properties were discovered (and economic decision maybe; iron hooks may have been better, but the ore was scarce and fishermen would be outbidded by warriors in any case?). Can bone items can be used to accurately model the kind of effects we need to circumvent with plastic nowadays?

Is there anything specially noxious about plastic, or is it the sheer magnitude of the material that is being dumped in the world what counts?

replies.title: Re: Subtle properties or plastic, and other materials
replies.content: To some extent, we simply don't know; we experience effects and try to come up with theories to explain them. Bone, leather, and other animal products are of course heavily used in traditional technologies and arts, and it would take a fair amount of research by people with well-developed clairvoyant capacities to get a clear sense of the difference.

replies.title: Re: Subtle properties or plastic, and other materials
replies.content:
I am only modestly psychic, but after about a year of doing devotions and natural magic on a home altar, I noticed that the steel filing cabinet I was using as an altar had picked up a heck of a charge, and that the glass-topped table that a witch and mage I knew was using regularly as her altar felt like nothing.

Then I did some reading about the use of crystals for magic. I generalized a conclusion that materials that have a regular molecular structure hold a charge more than materials that are amorphous. Probably the more regular the structure, the more effectively it absorbs and holds a charge, which would explain why rock crystal and crystalline gems are reputed to be especially powerful, igneous and metamorphic rocks more powerful than sedimentary ones, some steels have more power than iron which has more power than copper (because hammering, reheating and quenching changes the molecular structure) and that metals in general hold a charge better than fired clay, which is a little stronger than green clay, which beats glass which beats plastic.

Looking at natural materials, most of the residual matter of formerly living beings has a cellular structure. Bone can hold a charge because of this, shell to a lesser degree, and hardwoods probably hold a charge better than softwood; leather can hold a bit of a charge, and so might tightly woven cloth, because the threads it is made of have some ordered cellular structure, further structure added in the spinning process and more in the weaving.

Amber is a magical substance even though it looks amorphous. It is hardened tree sap and holds a physical electrical charge. Jet is extra hard coal. It was mourning jewelry in the nineteenth century because of its color, but perhaps also because of the concentrated death energy of the compressed plants in it. I believe, though I have not experimented, that jet has electrical properties that are different from amber's. Some Wiccan priestesses wear necklaces of alternating amber and jet beads. Apart from whatever electrical or subtle currents this arrangement creates, the amber is concentrated solar energy both by color and by the fact that it is the fossilized life blood (sap) of a plant, whereas jet is telluric. Alternating symbols of light and darkness, life and death, in a circle are appropriate Wiccan symbolism.

I never use plastic in magic if I can avoid it, but the suggestion JMG made about its inherent concentrated death energy is plausible.

replies.title: Re: Occult and Combat
replies.content:
There used to be quite an overlap between Western occultism and strength training, and that overlapped into such Western martial arts as boxing and fencing. In my experience, you can combine combat arts very well with occult training, as long as the combat arts don't have the kind of breath-oriented subtle energy work you find (for example) in Chinese qigong. That I know of, the arts you've mentioned are entirely free of such things, as (for that matter) are traditional Japanese martial arts.

As for vegetarianism, one of the big divides in traditional Western occultism is between the schools that teach vegetarianism and the schools that teach ceremonial magic. If you do serious magical workings, you need to eat meat -- its grounding influence keeps you from becoming hypersensitive in ways that can be dangerous to the operative mage. If you're vegetarian, you're better off practicing a form of occultism that doesn't include magical practices, such as the one Heindel taught (and his school still teaches).

comments.content:
Good evening JMG. In the September Open Post comment thread we had a brief conversation about some of my experiences with a river, that ended with you saying, “It sounds beautiful and, from a Druidical perspective, holy.” The open post week was over before I had a chance to reply:

I quite agree. Which is why I’ve been asking nervous questions about nature spirits from time to time over the past few months. Might as well fill you in on the background, if you don’t mind a long late post.

Last spring, my wife and I were looking for a new home in southeast Massachusetts closer to where her side of the family lives. We were looking for a nice affordable place in one of the few remaining “sketchy” towns (meaning un-gentrified, where working-class people still live and no one moves there for the schools) in the region. What we stumbled on was all that and a bit more.

The house is small and one side of our yard borders on the chain link and barbed wire fence that surrounds the town sewage treatment plant. Highways and a railroad crowd in nearby. Yet most of what’s actually visible is forest, marsh reeds, and a half mile stretch of riverine estuary that practically flows past our front door. It’s one of those tiny pocket paradises among surroundings that look utterly hopeless on a map.

More important, we both felt two things at first visit: that we belong here, and that we’re welcome here. The first from inside, the second from outside. (Both felt completely different from the usual crude egotism- and fear-targeted workings of the real estate agents.) Comparing our individual experiences afterward was a TSW moment, as in: This Site Wills. There was and still is some doubt about whether we deserve such favor, but here we are.

In any case, living in such an absurdly liminal and possibly sacred place called for a different approach than my usual “live and let live” detachment. Ignoring such an expressive genius loci would seem to risk either the spirit losing interest, or its influence acting on me without my knowing. So, following some suggestions discussed here a few months ago, I first formally introduced myself to the place a few days after I arrived (as soon as the exigencies of the actual move, which was delayed until the start of September, permitted). I sat on a rock under a tree at the very edge of the river, where I could watch the waterline inch closer between the reeds and cattails on the rising tide. Over the course of about an hour, I greeted aloud each living thing I saw or heard; and the sun, wind, water, stone, and soil; and the corresponding elements within myself. After repeating this observance a few times, it’s becoming a habit to just pause to acknowledge these things as I go about my regular activities. The results so far are as I described them before.

One side effect, my moods and energy now tend to follow the tides. A bit of research suggests that this is not an uncommon experience. Observing and addressing the rising and setting sun moderates that effect, but sometimes I get lazy, or can’t schedule it.

Now, you probably noticed that this means I’m living about four feet above sea level in a rapidly warming climate. I know we won’t be passing this “property” down to my grand-nephews, but to the ocean instead.

Of course the river knows this too. It has ample means to sense the composition of the atmosphere, and such changes have happened many times before.

I have a strong and growing suspicion that talking about “preserving” or “protecting” or “restoring” parts of the natural world that are facing unavoidable change is having the same sort of distancing effect, the same closing off of meaningful communication, as the vapid chatter about “getting better” and “doctors who work wonders now” we subject mortally ill people to when they’re trying to get ready to pass on. ( I’ve seen people light up with appreciation upon hearing an honest, “How do you feel about dying?” I'm guessing you have too.)

So one of the things I’ve said to the land and the river is that I understand they’ll become part of the ocean in a mere few decades, perhaps about the same time I’ll be passing on and becoming something else myself. But there will be coastlines and estuaries somewhere, and the life there then will descend from the life here now. (Holy enough for me, that.)

Positive reactions so far, anyhow, as previously noted. Good day today. Cut strangling vines off an oak tree. (Didn’t ask its permission first. Oops. Dropped my wedding ring right off my finger into the underbrush in the process, without noticing until later. My wife helped search for it and found it. Fortunately for me, she chose to give it back to me.) A kingfisher arrived and successfully (auspiciously) fished. Everything’s preparing for winter.

Feeling my way. Without changing my overall approach, which seems to be working well, what should I be doing or understanding better?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Don't ask me; ask the land and the river!
